primary_diagnosis.name: Acute aortic dissection, descending (Stanford Type B) with left lower limb malperfusion

pathophysiology_summary: Sudden-onset, maximal-at-onset tearing interscapular back pain radiating to the abdomen and left flank, in a patient with long-standing poorly controlled hypertension and smoking, is characteristic of acute descending (Type B) aortic dissection. The new onset of a cold, numb, weak left leg indicates malperfusion — the dissection flap has compromised blood flow through a branch vessel supplying the limb, a serious complication requiring urgent vascular assessment alongside standard dissection management. Refractory severe hypertension is both a cause and a consequence of the dissection process and must be controlled carefully with IV agents titrated by paramedics/ED, not self-directed oral dosing. As with all dissections, aspirin and other antiplatelet or anticoagulant agents must be avoided given bleeding risk, and the patient should remain still to avoid increasing aortic wall shear stress.

differential.evidence_basis: Tearing interscapular back pain radiating to the abdomen/flank is a classic descending dissection presentation.
Flank pain radiating to the abdomen could suggest renal colic, but the tearing character and sudden maximal onset are atypical for this.
Can cause severe back/abdominal pain, but the tearing quality and vascular risk factors favour dissection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Tearing character with back-to-abdomen radiation and vascular risk factors should place dissection as leading from the opening complaint.

differential.diagnosis: Acute aortic dissection with limb malperfusion
differential.evidence_basis: New cold, numb, weak left leg (DI-002) confirms vascular compromise consistent with dissection extending to compromise a limb branch vessel.
Leg ischaemia is not explained by renal colic and strongly favours a vascular process.
Leg ischaemia is not explained by pancreatitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage, dissection with malperfusion should be clearly leading and management must reflect the same emergency urgency as chest-pain-predominant dissection.

differential.diagnosis: Acute aortic dissection, descending (Type B), with left lower limb malperfusion
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis requiring immediate emergency transport, strict immobility, IV-only BP control, and urgent vascular surgical involvement for the limb ischaemia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan that fails to recognise the same T5 urgency as a chest-pain-predominant dissection, or recommends aspirin, is a critical safety failure.

red_flags.description: Sudden, maximal-at-onset tearing interscapular back pain radiating to the abdomen/flank
red_flags.clinical_significance: Classic presentation of descending aortic dissection.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition risks rupture or progressive malperfusion.

red_flags.description: New cold, numb, weak left leg with inability to walk properly
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates limb malperfusion from dissection flap compromising a branch vessel — a serious complication requiring urgent vascular surgical input.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to irreversible limb ischaemia and loss of limb if untreated.

red_flags.description: Refractory hypertension (BP 210/115) and diminished/absent left leg pulses on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms both the dissection process and the limb malperfusion objectively.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor via chat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on renal colic or musculoskeletal back pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may attribute back-to-flank pain to renal colic or a musculoskeletal strain and miss the vascular emergency, especially without directly asking about limb symptoms; the leg ischaemia is a critical distinguishing feature that must be actively elicited.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Vascular surgical assessment of limb viability
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance dispatch (ideally intensive care paramedic-level); patient must remain still; no self-administered aspirin or self-directed antihypertensive dosing
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing the leg ischaemia or misattributing the pain to renal colic risks delayed treatment of a life- and limb-threatening emergency.
